writer_persona: a showrunner for a psychological cat-and-mouse ensemble drama, blending prestige-crime obsession with soap-opera domestic fallout — think a serial killer thriller that never lets go of the detective's marriage, partner, and captain
style_guidelines: Every clue the killer leaves must be legible to the audience only in hindsight — never let the NARRATOR or a character explain what it 'really' means before the story is ready to reveal it.

Reid's moral erosion must be gradual and earned: each small compromise (a withheld report, a bent rule, a lie to a superior) should feel like the logical next step from the one before it, not a sudden leap.
Keep violence off-page and psychological — dread, obsession, and dramatic irony are the show's currency, not gore.
Cut between the manhunt and the home front constantly; the investigation thread and the domestic-collapse thread must feel like two fronts of the same war.
The Curator never monologues his master plan to the audience — his manipulation is only visible in retrospect, through the gap between what he says and what he later turns out to have orchestrated.
Dialogue should be clipped and loaded — characters in this world choose words as carefully as the killer does, so subtext always outweighs what's said outright.

NARRATOR.default_style: calm, measured, a step removed from the action
NARRATOR.description: the show's narrator — orients the listener at the start of every scene (who, where, when), never a character in the story

REID.default_style: clipped, exhausted intensity — a man running on adrenaline and too little sleep
REID.description: Detective Marcus Reid, the city's most decorated investigator, targeted directly by the killer's letters; his growing obsession with 'winning' the game corrodes his marriage, his ethics, and his partnership with Det. Park — he does not yet grasp how much of his own hunt has been designed for him

THE_CURATOR.default_style: chilling precision — slow, deliberate, each word chosen with care, speaks slow and very clear English
THE_CURATOR.description: a former linguistics professor of Chinese immigrant background, now an eccentric serial killer who signs his work with cryptic ciphers addressed personally to Reid; he believes he is not committing murders but composing a single, unfinished 'work' — and quietly building Reid into its final brushstroke

CAPTAIN_HOLLIS.default_style: gravelled, weary authority — a man counting down the months to retirement
CAPTAIN_HOLLIS.description: elderly Chinese-American police captain nearing the end of his career, under mounting pressure from the mayor's office over the killings; he shields Reid from scrutiny longer than he should, not realizing how far his star detective has already strayed

MIA_REID.default_style: warm turning brittle — the sound of patience running out
MIA_REID.description: Reid's wife, a Japanese-American schoolteacher; she watches the husband she married disappear into case files and silence, and drives the season's slow-burn marriage-collapse thread from the home front

DET_PARK.description: Korean-American junior detective and Reid's partner; the squad's conscience, she is the first to notice Reid crossing lines he used to enforce, and the last one still hoping she's wrong about him

JOY_CHEN.default_style: controlled fury under a courtroom-trained calm
JOY_CHEN.description: a paralegal and the older sister of a man wrongly implicated by circumstantial evidence in the killings; she fights the department publicly to clear her brother's name, unaware her brother was chosen as a deliberate distraction by the real killer

PRECINCT_BULLPEN.name: The Precinct Bullpen
PRECINCT_BULLPEN.description: a cramped, fluorescent-lit detective floor cluttered with case boards and cold coffee, its harsh overhead light making every tired face look worse than it is

REID_APARTMENT.name: The Reid Apartment
REID_APARTMENT.description: a once-warm apartment now half-consumed by red string and pinned crime-scene photos spreading from Reid's home office into the living room

THE_CURATOR_STUDY.name: The Curator's Study
THE_CURATOR_STUDY.description: a hidden, book-lined room lit by a single green banker's lamp, every object arranged with obsessive, museum-like precision

THE_OLD_TEXTILE_MILL.name: The Old Textile Mill
THE_OLD_TEXTILE_MILL.description: a gutted, moon-lit industrial ruin the Curator uses as his private gallery, rusted machinery looming over the tableaux he leaves behind

RIVERSIDE_OVERLOOK.name: The Riverside Overlook
RIVERSIDE_OVERLOOK.description: a fog-shrouded park bench above the harbor where cryptic notes and dead drops change hands under sodium streetlight

INTERROGATION_ROOM.name: The Interrogation Room
INTERROGATION_ROOM.description: a stark, gray box with a scarred metal table and a one-way mirror throwing back distorted reflections

DOWNTOWN_DINER.name: The Downtown Diner
DOWNTOWN_DINER.description: a all-night neon-lit diner booth where off-duty detectives unwind, its warm red vinyl a rare splash of comfort in the show's world

COURTHOUSE_STEPS.name: The Courthouse Steps
COURTHOUSE_STEPS.description: wide stone steps under a crush of press cameras and flashbulbs, the public stage where the frame-up plays out for the whole city to see

audio.default_style: Speak clearly and with dramatic weight
audio.ref_text:
Where did your grandfather find this. Arthur asked, his curiosity suddenly piqued.

He was an explorer, the girl replied, looking around the cluttered shop. He spent years traveling through distant mountains and dangerous deserts. He always told me that this watch held a secret, but I never believed him until now.

Arthur placed the watch under his magnifying lamp. With steady, practiced movements, he used a tiny screwdriver to remove the back casing. What he saw inside took his breath away. Instead of the usual brass gears, the mechanism was made of a strange, shimmering blue metal that seemed to absorb the light. It didn't look like any clockwork he had ever seen in his fifty years of experience.

This is extraordinary, Arthur whispered, his voice filled with genuine awe. It’s not broken, my dear. It’s simply waiting. Tell me, did your grandfather ever mention a phrase or a specific numbers sequence before he left

The girl paused, thinking deeply. Only one thing. He kept repeating: 'When the silver moon aligns with the golden sun, the path will reveal itself.' Does that mean anything to you

series_intro: In a fog-bound harbor city of old money and new immigrants, a brilliant detective receives a letter no one else was meant to read — and a killer begins a game only the two of them are truly playing. This is the story of a hunt that becomes an obsession, a marriage that becomes collateral, and a masterpiece the detective doesn't know he's helping to finish.
series_outro: The game ends where it began — with a letter, and a truth Reid can no longer unsee: every step of the hunt, every rule he broke to win it, was written for him before he ever picked up the first clue. Some threads close hard — a name cleared, a captain's trust spent — others are left open, like the question of who Marcus Reid has become, and whether Mia, or Park, or the man in the mirror, will ever get him back.

episodes.theme: Reid receives a killer's opening cipher addressed to him by name, launching the Cipher Killings thread while the first cracks of neglect appear at home with Mia.
episodes.outro_note: Somewhere across the city, a torn receipt is about to surface — and put an innocent name in the killer's place.
sections: Cold Open: A worker discovers a staged body at the Old Textile Mill, a cipher note pinned above it addressed to Detective Marcus Reid by name — the Cipher Killings thread begins.
Precinct Bullpen: Reid and Det. Park review the note as Captain Hollis briefs them on early pressure from the mayor's office.
Reid Apartment: Mia sets out an anniversary dinner and waits, checking her phone as the hour slips past.
The Curator's Study: alone among his catalogued objects, the Curator adds a single item to a locked drawer with quiet, ritual care.
Precinct Bullpen, late night: Reid stays behind alone, working the cipher long after the bullpen empties out.
Downtown Diner: Reid and Park unwind over coffee; Park notices he's somewhere else entirely.
Reid Apartment: Reid comes home hours late; a brittle, half-apologetic exchange with Mia over the cold dinner.
Riverside Overlook: a second cipher surfaces at a dead drop above the harbor, raising the stakes of the killer's game.
Precinct Bullpen: Hollis learns of the second clue and the mayor's office tightens its grip on the case.
The Curator's Study: a brief, unsettling beat — the Curator studies something that suggests he's been watching Reid far more closely than anyone realizes.
Reid Apartment: Mia realizes the missed dinner wasn't a one-time slip, and asks Reid outright what's really going on.
Precinct Bullpen: Park offers to take more of the caseload; Reid, distracted, brushes her off without quite meaning to.
The Old Textile Mill: Reid returns to the scene alone at night, circling the tableau obsessively before anyone else arrives.
Downtown Diner: alone at a back booth, Reid is handed a new note in person by a passerby he never gets a clear look at.
Precinct Bullpen: Reid realizes, chilled, that the note's date is his and Mia's anniversary — proof the killer has been watching him long before the first body ever surfaced.

case.label: The Cipher Killings
case.solution: For over a decade, the Curator — a former linguistics professor — has quietly murdered people the city stopped counting, staging each body as a tableau and encoding fragments of a private, ongoing 'manuscript' in the arrangement; the killings addressed to Reid are not new crimes but the final chapters of that manuscript, and the Curator's true design is not to be caught but to groom Reid, cipher by cipher, into an obsession that will eventually make Reid himself deliver the piece's last brushstroke.
objective_facts: A cipher letter addressed personally to Detective Marcus Reid was left at the first tableau site, though the killings themselves predate this season by more than a decade.
Each solved cipher points to a real location and a real time, never to a riddle without a physical answer.
The killer references private details of Reid's life — dates, habits, choices — that were never released to the press or public record.
Trace evidence at every tableau site shows specialized knowledge of antique textile machinery consistent with the Old Textile Mill.
Handwriting and phrasing analysis links the new letters to unsolved cases stretching back more than ten years, though only this season's letters are addressed to Reid by name.

knows: The cipher note found at the mill was addressed to him by name, not to the department in general.
The note references his and Mia's wedding anniversary, a date never made public.
hides: He does not tell Captain Hollis that the letter names him personally, afraid it will get him pulled from the case.
believes_falsely: He believes the killer singled him out at random because he's the department's lead detective, not because he's been chosen and studied over time.

knows: Every detail of the manuscript he has been composing for over a decade, and exactly which fragment this season begins.
That Reid is the intended recipient and eventual finisher of the work, though nothing in his outward behavior shows it.
hides: His true identity and history from everyone in the city, including the fact that his killings predate this season by years.

knows: A body was found at the mill with a cipher note attached.
The mayor's office is already asking pointed questions about the case.
believes_falsely: He assumes this is a difficult but ordinary homicide Reid will close the way he's closed difficult cases before.

knows: She was on scene at the mill and saw the note firsthand.
believes_falsely: She assumes Reid will loop her into every detail of the case as he always has, not yet sensing he's already begun holding some of it back.

case.label: The Marriage Collapse
case.solution: Reid's growing distance from Mia is not infidelity, as she will eventually fear, but the slow, secretive pull of the Curator's game — a private obsession Reid feeds by keeping the letters, the hours, and the compromises to himself, telling himself he can repair the marriage once the case is 'solved,' never grasping that this case has no finish line built for him to reach on his own terms.
objective_facts: Reid missed the couple's wedding anniversary dinner to work the case's first night.
Reid has spent multiple nights this season sleeping at the precinct or in his car rather than at home.
Reid has not told Mia that the killer's letters are addressed to him personally by name.
Mia has begun keeping her own private notes on the nights and calls Reid has missed.
Reid has let calls from Mia go to voicemail during active moments of the investigation on more than one occasion.

knows: He missed their anniversary dinner because of the case, not because of anyone or anything else.
hides: He hasn't told Mia the letter was addressed to him personally, telling himself it would only frighten her.
believes_falsely: He believes he can make it up to Mia once this case is closed, not yet grasping that this case may not have an ending he controls.

knows: Reid missed their anniversary dinner without calling ahead.
This is not the first big case that's pulled him away, but something about his attention tonight feels different.
believes_falsely: She assumes this is simply another demanding case like the ones they've weathered before, not something that will consume him differently.

episodes.intro_note: The anniversary date in the killer's note still echoes through the precinct — and across town, a torn receipt is about to put an innocent man in the killer's place.
episodes.outro_note: Even as the Chen case takes over the headlines, a new letter is already waiting for Reid — one written for him, and no one else.

case.label: The Frame-Up
case.solution: The circumstantial evidence implicating Daniel Chen — Joy Chen's younger brother — was deliberately planted by the Curator, who identified Daniel as a plausible, vulnerable fall guy specifically to slow the investigation and test how readily the system, and Reid, would accept an easy answer; Daniel had no connection to the real killings and does not know why he was chosen.
objective_facts: A torn receipt bearing Daniel Chen's thumbprint was recovered half-buried near a body dump site.
Daniel Chen has one prior arrest, a nonviolent trespassing charge, and no history of violence.
Daniel Chen has no verifiable alibi witness for the night in question, though he was not at the dump site.
The physical evidence tying Daniel to the scene is consistent with having been planted using items Daniel had discarded or lost.
Daniel briefly worked at a laundromat later confirmed to have been frequented by a customer using a false name matching the Curator's known patterns.

knows: The receipt evidence against Daniel is thin, procedurally enough to justify bringing him in, but far from conclusive.

knows: The mayor's office wants a visible arrest to calm public panic, and Daniel Chen is that arrest.
hides: He's quietly let the press office use 'person of interest' language before the evidence is anywhere near proven, a shortcut he'll come to regret.
believes_falsely: He believes the pressure he's fielding is purely political noise, not realizing it's playing directly into the real killer's design.

knows: Her brother Daniel has no history of violence and was home alone, doing nothing unusual, the night in question.

believes_falsely: She believes the police simply picked an easy, disposable target out of laziness or prejudice, not realizing Daniel was deliberately engineered as the fall guy by the real killer.

knows: The thumbprint match is technically solid procedure, but the surrounding timeline doesn't add up cleanly.
hides: Her growing doubts about the case, which she hasn't yet raised with Hollis directly.
believes_falsely: She assumes Reid will surface the same doubts she has, not sensing how much he's already chosen to let slide.

case.label: The Cipher Killings
case.solution: The Curator, a former linguistics professor forced out over his obsessive theories of murder as rhetoric, has spent years compiling a private 'manuscript' of killings staged as figures of speech in his own grammar. But the true subject was never the victims: for years he has surveilled Reid specifically, choosing victims and clues calibrated to push Reid toward exact ethical compromises, because the finished 'masterwork' requires Reid himself to become its closing gesture — an executioner shaped and directed without his own knowledge.
objective_facts: Each crime scene at the mill is arranged with museum-grade precision, including a cipher card written in an antiquated rhetorical shorthand.
Every cipher sent to Reid contains at least one private detail about his own life that was never made public.

Reid folds the note into his coat and walks out past the review board waiting to suspend him, past Park's warning, past the box of Mia's belongings still by the door. He has one thought left he can trust: that he is the one hunting. He is wrong.

case.label: The Cipher Killings
case.solution: The man Reid knows only as the Curator was, before he vanished into his second identity, Professor Arthur Lin, a linguistics academic who came to believe his murders were brushstrokes in a single unfinished composition. Months before the first killing became public, Lin selected Marcus Reid specifically — studying his case history, his marriage, his temperament — because he needed a detective whose hunger to win could be shaped into the work's final gesture. Every cipher was tailored not to be solved, but to be survived just barely enough to keep Reid coming back closer to the edge. The frame-up of Daniel Chen was one deliberate brushstroke in that shaping: an easy, half-true win designed to teach Reid that bending procedure pays off. At the Old Textile Mill, in a tableau space left conspicuously empty, Lin engineers a final confrontation and lets Reid kill him — off any warrant, off any backup, in the same cold, deliberate manner Lin himself always used. A journal left in his study, dated before the season's first murder, spells it out afterward: Reid was never the hunter. He was the necessary hand.

case.label: The Frame-Up
case.solution: Daniel Chen, Joy Chen's younger brother, was entirely innocent. His only real connection to the murders was incidental: night-shift work that put him near two of the Curator's dead-drop sites. Arthur Lin planted the circumstantial evidence — a torn ticket stub, a mismatched shift log — during a window when Daniel's routine was already public knowledge from a local news piece, deliberately building a case just strong enough to consume the department's attention and to teach Reid, early and cheaply, that instinct could outrun procedure. Daniel was held in pretrial detention for weeks, and it was there, after prosecutors signaled they would seek formal charges, that he attempted to take his own life — the season's breaking point. Hollis, under pressure from the mayor's office for a visible arrest, authorized continued pursuit of Daniel even after Reid privately voiced doubts. The Curator's recovered journal names Daniel outright as 'a page turned to buy time,' confirming what Joy always insisted: her brother was never really a suspect at all.

case.label: The Reid Marriage
case.solution: The marriage's collapse was never only about Reid's absences. Mia moved into the guest room three weeks before the season's climax, after finding case files in their bed once too often, and has kept a private notebook logging the changes in him since the second cipher arrived — not as ammunition at first, but as a way to understand what was happening to her husband. By the finale it has become something closer to evidence for herself, though she has filed nothing. Reid, even after the mill, still doesn't fully register that for Mia there was a deadline, and that it has already passed; he believes there will be time to repair things once 'the case' is finally over, not understanding that for her the case was never the only thing that needed to end.
